refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent verification and clearer traceability to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to input parameters and stakeholder requirements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission inputs.
FR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance targets in a retrievable format.
FR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance target baselines and calibration updates.
FR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.

candidate_dps: DP1: Capture worksite mission context (operational objectives, environmental constraints, safety policies) via structured intake process; parse into a normalized context model; apply context-to-performance mapping rules to derive candidate performance targets; validate targets against safety/policy constraints; document traceability chain from context inputs to each target.

DP2: Maintain a parameterized performance baseline repository indexed by worksite profile (mission type, environment class, operational mode); retrieve applicable baseline for current worksite; apply delta adjustments based on mission-specific inputs; generate performance targets as instantiation of baseline + deltas; log all adjustments with timestamps and rationale.

DP3: Implement a rule-based inference engine that accepts worksite context as facts (mission objectives, hazard classes, operational policies); execute domain rules (e.g., 'if hazard_class=high AND mission=precision_task THEN target_accuracy=±X%') to synthesize performance requirements; output derived targets with rule provenance; maintain audit trail of rule firings.

DP4: Decompose worksite mission into functional sub-tasks; assign performance criticality to each sub-task based on safety impact and operational priority; aggregate sub-task requirements into system-level performance targets; trace each target back to originating sub-task and safety justification.

DP5: Establish a multi-stage validation pipeline: (1) parse worksite inputs for completeness and consistency; (2) cross-check derived targets against historical baselines and outlier detection; (3) validate targets against worksite safety plan constraints; (4) obtain stakeholder sign-off; (5) archive validated targets with full lineage metadata.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Capture and normalize worksite mission context through structured intake; apply transparent context-to-performance mapping rules to derive candidate targets; validate derived targets against safety/policy constraints and historical consistency; document full traceability from context inputs through mapping logic to final performance targets; maintain versioned baseline history with calibration/update audit trail.

potential_sources: DP1 assumes availability of well-defined worksite context inputs (mission objectives, safety plan, operational policies); if inputs are ambiguous or incomplete, mapping rules may produce invalid targets. Mitigation: enforce structured intake schema with validation gates.

DP5 validation stage depends on access to historical baselines and stakeholder availability; delays in sign-off could block target finalization. Mitigation: define asynchronous validation workflow with provisional target release and post-hoc approval.
Coupling between context capture (DP1) and validation (DP5) is intentional and loose: context is captured independently; validation is applied as a separate stage. No circular dependencies detected.
DP1 and DP5 do not prescribe specific technologies (e.g., database, rules engine, UI); solution remains neutral and can be realized across multiple implementation strategies.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP cleanly separates concerns: (1) context normalization, (2) logical mapping, (3) constraint validation, (4) documentation/traceability. Each concern can be designed and tested independently. The DP is solution-neutral and supports multiple realization paths (e.g., spreadsheet-based, database-driven, or microservice-based implementations).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in a form that can be captured and normalized (e.g., documented safety plan, operational procedures, mission brief). RISK: If context is implicit, oral, or inconsistently documented, intake process will fail or produce incomplete models. MITIGATION: Establish worksite context documentation standards and intake checklist.

ASSUMPTION: A set of context-to-performance mapping rules exists or can be elicited from domain experts (safety engineers, operational planners). RISK: If rules are unavailable or poorly understood, derived targets may be incorrect or unjustifiable. MITIGATION: Conduct domain expert interviews; document rules with examples and rationale; validate rules against historical mission data.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formally documented and accessible. RISK: If policies are outdated, conflicting, or not enforced, validation stage may approve unsafe targets. MITIGATION: Require policy review and sign-off as part of intake; flag policy conflicts for resolution before target derivation.

ASSUMPTION: Stakeholders (safety officer, mission planner, system operator) are available for sign-off within acceptable timeframe. RISK: Delays in approval could block deployment or force use of provisional targets. MITIGATION: Define escalation path and provisional release criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Historical baseline data and calibration records are available and trustworthy. RISK: If baselines are missing, outdated, or corrupted, validation and outlier detection will be unreliable. MITIGATION: Establish baseline curation and versioning discipline; implement data integrity checks.

ASSUMPTION: Traceability metadata (context inputs, mapping rules applied, validation results, approvals) can be captured and maintained without excessive overhead. RISK: If documentation burden is high, teams may skip or falsify records. MITIGATION: Automate metadata capture where possible; integrate traceability into workflow tools.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly require: (1) a structured worksite context intake schema (list of mandatory and optional context fields); (2) a documented set of context-to-performance mapping rules with domain justification; (3) a validation checklist that includes safety plan alignment, policy compliance, and historical consistency checks; (4) a traceability metadata model that links each performance target to its originating context inputs, mapping rules, and validation results. This will clarify the scope of DP1+DP5 and enable measurable acceptance criteria (e.g., 'all performance targets have documented traceability to at least one context input and one mapping rule'; 'all targets pass safety plan validation before release').

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized intake, context normalization, transparent mapping rule engine, target derivation, constraint validation, traceability documentation, and versioned baseline management for worksite mission context and performance targets.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive worksite mission context data, environmental parameters, and historical performance records for intake and normalization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Emit derived candidate performance targets and validated final targets to downstream execution systems (Vehicle, Teleoperation).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Query safety/policy constraints for validation; emit alerts if derived targets violate constraints or historical consistency checks.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the logical home for intake, normalization, rule-based mapping, and traceability. Context-to-performance derivation is a core planning/reasoning function. Minimal diffusion: only exports validated targets via Tasking; imports context via Telemetry; queries constraints via SafetyAlert. Versioned baseline and audit trail are internal state management.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns constraint validation and policy enforcement, but does not own intake, normalization, or mapping logic. DP1+DP5 requires end-to-end ownership of context→target derivation. Placing the full DP here would fragment the mapping logic and require Safety Services to own non-safety-specific reasoning, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a planning or reasoning module. Intake, normalization, and mapping logic belong upstream in the decision layer. Placing DP1+DP5 in Vehicle would scatter mission planning across the platform and create tight coupling between planning and hardware.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is an execution/control interface for human operators. It consumes targets but does not own intake, normalization, or mapping. Placing DP1+DP5 here would conflate planning with operator control, reducing modularity and reusability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the integrated DP1+DP5 because it is the mission planning and reasoning layer. It receives raw worksite context (Telemetry), applies transparent mapping rules to derive targets, validates against constraints (SafetyAlert), maintains traceability and versioned baselines, and emits validated targets (Tasking). This keeps all context-to-performance logic in one cohesive module, minimizes diffusion, and preserves clear separation between planning (NGHE), execution (Vehicle, Teleoperation), and safety governance (Safety Services).
containment_risks: If Safety Services later requires ownership of constraint validation logic, a secondary interface (SafetyAlert) must remain bidirectional to allow NGHE to query and receive policy updates without duplicating validation rules.

If worksite context normalization rules become highly domain-specific or vehicle-dependent, NGHE may need to accept vehicle-specific context adapters via Telemetry to avoid tight coupling to Vehicle internals.

Versioned baseline and audit trail storage may require external persistence; ensure NGHE does not become a monolithic data store. Consider externalizing audit logs via a separate interface if audit volume grows.
Traceability documentation (context→mapping→target) must remain human-readable and queryable; if this becomes a bottleneck, consider a dedicated traceability service interface.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria explicitly require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baseline history. NGHE Solution's IM boundary includes centralized intake, transparent mapping rule engine, traceability documentation, and versioned baseline management—all directly measurable against these criteria. Verification can be performed by: (1) auditing traceability logs for completeness of context→mapping→target chains; (2) validating baseline versioning and calibration audit trail; (3) confirming all derived targets are documented with source context and mapping rule references.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to NGHE Solution is justified by its role as the mission planning and reasoning layer. The IM boundary explicitly owns end-to-end context-to-performance derivation, eliminating ambiguity about which module is responsible for intake, normalization, mapping logic, constraint validation, and traceability. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert bidirectional) are well-defined, reducing cross-module ownership disputes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP5 (integrated) directly addresses the FR requirement to 'derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.' The DP specifies a complete logical solution: (1) capture and normalize worksite mission context (DP1 intake); (2) apply transparent context-to-performance mapping rules (DP5 derivation); (3) validate against safety/policy constraints (DP5 validation); (4) document full traceability (DP5 traceability); (5) maintain versioned baseline history with audit trail (DP5 baseline management). The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific algorithms or tools) and is sufficiently detailed to guide IM design. No logical gaps detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert bidirectional interface creates a secondary dependency: if Safety Services later owns constraint validation logic, NGHE must remain the primary orchestrator of context→target derivation to avoid duplicating mapping rules. Mitigation: establish clear ownership of constraint *definition* (Safety Services) vs. constraint *application* (NGHE) in interface contract.

Worksite context normalization rules may become vehicle-dependent or domain-specific over time. If Vehicle-specific adapters are required, ensure they are pluggable via Telemetry interface rather than hardcoded in NGHE. Risk: tight coupling between NGHE and Vehicle internals. Mitigation: define a context adapter pattern and version it explicitly.

Versioned baseline and audit trail storage could grow into a monolithic data store within NGHE. If audit volume or query complexity increases, consider externalizing audit logs to a dedicated persistence service via a new interface (e.g., 'AuditLog'). Current IM boundary assumes internal state management; monitor for scalability.

Traceability documentation (context→mapping→target) must remain human-readable and queryable for compliance and debugging. If traceability becomes a bottleneck (e.g., large-scale worksite deployments with high-frequency target updates), consider a dedicated traceability service interface. Current IM assumes NGHE owns documentation; validate this assumption under load.

DP5 'transparent context-to-performance mapping rules' implies rules are explicit and auditable. If mapping rules are embedded in code or learned models, transparency may be compromised. Mitigation: enforce rule externalization (e.g., rule engine, configuration files) and version control for all mapping logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent fit (0.92 containment score). NGHE Solution is the natural owner of DP1+DP5 because it is the mission planning and reasoning layer. All core responsibilities—intake, normalization, mapping, validation, traceability, baseline management—are cohesively contained within NGHE's IM boundary. Minimal diffusion (0.08): only exports validated targets via Tasking, imports context via Telemetry, and queries constraints via SafetyAlert. No responsibilities are scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Separation of concerns is preserved: NGHE owns planning logic; Vehicle owns execution; Safety Services owns constraint governance; Teleoperation owns operator control. The IM boundary is well-scoped and defensible.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract to explicitly distinguish between constraint *definition* (owned by Safety Services) and constraint *application* (owned by NGHE). Document the query/response protocol and versioning strategy for policy updates.

Define a context adapter pattern for vehicle-specific or domain-specific normalization rules. Ensure adapters are pluggable via Telemetry interface and versioned independently from core NGHE logic to prevent tight coupling.

Add acceptance criteria for traceability: specify the required granularity of context→mapping→target documentation (e.g., per-target, per-rule, per-update), the format (e.g., JSON, audit log), and the queryability requirements (e.g., search by context field, mapping rule, timestamp).
Establish a monitoring plan for audit trail growth and traceability query performance. Define thresholds for externalizing audit logs or traceability to a dedicated service interface.

Enforce rule externalization for all context-to-performance mapping logic. Prohibit embedding rules in code or learned models; require explicit rule definitions (e.g., rule engine, configuration files) that are version-controlled and auditable.
Document the versioned baseline management strategy: specify how baselines are created, tagged, compared, and rolled back. Include calibration/update audit trail format and retention policy.
Add a secondary IM boundary review after initial deployment to validate that diffusion risks (especially SafetyAlert bidirectionality and context adapter coupling) do not materialize in practice.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence and clear allocation. The FR is well-defined, the DP is logically sound and solution-agnostic, and the IM boundary is well-scoped with excellent containment (0.92). However, the gate analysis identifies five material diffusion risks and seven recommended revisions that must be resolved before decomposition can proceed safely. These risks are not blockers to the current level but are critical to address at the next level of detail. Specifically: (1) SafetyAlert bidirectionality creates secondary ownership ambiguity between constraint definition and application; (2) context adapter coupling risks tight binding to vehicle internals; (3) audit trail scalability and traceability queryability are unvalidated under load; (4) rule externalization and transparency enforcement are not yet formalized; (5) baseline versioning strategy lacks explicit documentation. These are architectural concerns that must be resolved through targeted revisions to DP5 and IM boundary specifications before sub-FRs can be reliably derived. A ZIGZAG_DOWN approach allows decomposition to proceed while enforcing these revisions as preconditions for sub-FR derivation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Capture and normalize worksite mission context from heterogeneous telemetry sources (vehicle state, environmental conditions, operator intent, policy constraints) into a canonical internal representation.
FR1.2: Apply transparent, auditable context-to-performance mapping rules to derive performance targets from normalized worksite context, with explicit rule versioning and change tracking.

FR1.3: Validate derived performance targets against active safety and policy constraints via SafetyAlert interface, with clear separation between constraint definition (Safety Services) and constraint application (NGHE).
FR1.4: Maintain versioned baseline history of performance targets with complete calibration and update audit trail, enabling rollback and compliance verification.
FR1.5: Document and expose full traceability chain (worksite context → mapping rule → performance target) in human-readable, queryable format for compliance, debugging, and operator transparency.

revise.why: DP5 ('transparent context-to-performance mapping rules') must be expanded to explicitly formalize: (1) rule externalization strategy (rule engine, configuration files, or declarative format) to prevent embedding rules in code or learned models; (2) SafetyAlert interface contract distinguishing constraint definition ownership (Safety Services) vs. constraint application ownership (NGHE), with explicit query/response protocol and policy versioning; (3) context adapter pattern for vehicle-specific normalization, with pluggable interface via Telemetry and independent versioning to prevent tight coupling; (4) traceability documentation granularity, format (JSON, audit log), and queryability requirements (search by context field, mapping rule, timestamp); (5) baseline versioning strategy including creation, tagging, comparison, rollback, and audit trail retention policy. These revisions are necessary to resolve diffusion risks and ensure sub-FRs can be derived with clear ownership boundaries and interface contracts.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the authoritative owner of context-to-performance derivation logic and will remain the primary orchestrator even as SafetyAlert bidirectionality increases.
Worksite context normalization can be abstracted into a pluggable adapter pattern without requiring deep coupling to vehicle internals.

Audit trail and traceability storage can be managed internally within NGHE IM boundary at current deployment scale; externalizing to a dedicated service is a future optimization, not a current requirement.
Mapping rules can be externalized (rule engine, configuration files) without compromising performance or real-time responsiveness of target derivation.

Safety Services will own constraint definition (policy, limits) and NGHE will own constraint application (validation logic); this separation is feasible and will be formalized in SafetyAlert interface contract.
Baseline versioning and calibration audit trail requirements are compatible with NGHE's internal state management and do not require external persistence services at current scale.
open_risks: SafetyAlert bidirectionality may create secondary ownership disputes if constraint application logic drifts into Safety Services or if constraint definition changes are not properly versioned and communicated to NGHE. Mitigation: formalize interface contract with explicit ownership boundaries and versioning strategy before sub-FR derivation.

Context adapter coupling may tighten over time if vehicle-specific normalization rules become complex or frequent. Risk: NGHE becomes tightly bound to vehicle internals, reducing modularity. Mitigation: enforce pluggable adapter pattern and monitor coupling metrics during initial deployment.

Audit trail and traceability storage may become a scalability bottleneck under high-frequency target updates or large-scale worksite deployments. Risk: query performance degrades, compliance audits become slow. Mitigation: establish monitoring thresholds and plan for externalizing to dedicated service if needed.

Rule externalization may introduce performance overhead if rule engine evaluation is not optimized. Risk: target derivation latency increases, impacting real-time responsiveness. Mitigation: benchmark rule engine performance and consider caching or pre-compilation strategies.

Traceability documentation may become verbose or difficult to query if granularity is not carefully specified. Risk: compliance audits are time-consuming, operators cannot easily understand target derivation rationale. Mitigation: define explicit granularity, format, and queryability requirements in revised DP5.

Baseline versioning strategy may be incompatible with rapid target updates or dynamic worksite conditions. Risk: baseline management becomes a bottleneck or introduces stale data. Mitigation: validate baseline versioning strategy under realistic worksite scenarios before sub-FR derivation.
evidence_needed: Formal SafetyAlert interface contract specifying constraint definition vs. application ownership, query/response protocol, policy versioning strategy, and error handling for constraint conflicts.
Context adapter pattern specification including pluggable interface design, versioning strategy, and examples of vehicle-specific normalization rules that can be implemented without tight coupling.

Traceability documentation specification including required granularity (per-target, per-rule, per-update), format (JSON schema, audit log structure), and queryability requirements (search capabilities, performance targets).
Baseline versioning strategy documentation including creation/tagging/comparison/rollback procedures, calibration audit trail format, and retention policy.
Rule externalization strategy including rule engine selection (or custom implementation), configuration file format, version control approach, and performance benchmarks.
Audit trail growth and traceability query performance monitoring plan with defined thresholds for externalizing to dedicated service.

Deployment validation plan confirming that diffusion risks (SafetyAlert bidirectionality, context adapter coupling, audit trail scalability, rule transparency) do not materialize in practice; recommend secondary IM boundary review after initial deployment.
